How is Urbanization a Driving Force for Change?

OpenSciEd Unit 4 · Lessons 1–2 · A teacher-facing guide showing how students use disciplinary literacy to read science texts, interpret data, build models, ask questions, investigate, and compare simulation results to authentic research.

Lesson FlowLiteracy StrategiesCase StudiesModelingDQBInvestigationGarden ExperimentLook-Fors
1

Teacher View of the Lesson Flow

1

Jigsaw Read

Students become “experts” on one case study by reading for patterns, interpreting charts, and identifying how urban and non-urban environments differ.

2

Share Evidence

Students explain their case to peers using scientific evidence, not just summary. This helps every group compare across species.

3

Model the System

Students create a model showing interactions among environments, traits, survival, reproduction, and population change.

4

Build the DQB

Students use QFT to generate questions that matter for explaining urbanization as a force for biological change.

5

Investigate & Compare

Students collect hawksbeard simulation data, then compare their explanation to the Garden Experiment scientific study.

Teacher context: The science goal is not memorizing facts about plants, birds, or rats. Students are learning how to read scientific evidence and use it to explain how environments can affect which traits or behaviors become more common in a population over time.
2

Where Disciplinary Literacy Shows Up

Interpreting Scientific Visuals

Students learn to read scientific graphs, mutation tables, proportions, diagrams, and models as sources of evidence rather than decorations.

Reading Like Scientists

Students read to identify the phenomenon, variables, patterns in data, and possible causal mechanisms.

Talking With Evidence

During the jigsaw, students translate graphs, tables, and text into claims they can explain to classmates.

Writing & Modeling Explanations

Students use models, data tables, proportions, and comparison writing to connect evidence to explanations.

Additional disciplinary literacy moves: Students synthesize information across multiple texts, compare evidence across organisms, interpret specialized scientific vocabulary in context, and support claims using quantitative and visual evidence.
Teacher move: Ask, “What does the data show?” before asking, “Why do you think that happened?”
Teacher move: Press students to point to a chart, table, or text detail when making a claim.
Teacher move: Help students distinguish observation from inference.
Teacher move: Encourage students to compare cases using common language: environment, trait, advantage, survival, reproduction, population.
3

Jigsaw Case Studies: Evidence Students Read and Interpret

Case Study A

Hawksbeard (Crepis sancta)

Literacy purpose: Students interpret a graph and connect seed structure to seed dispersal in cities.

  • Feathery seeds disperse farther.
  • Non-feathery seeds fall near the parent plant.
  • Urban plants show a higher proportion of non-feathery seeds.
Case Study A photo: hawksbeard data and background reading.
Case Study B

Dark-Eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis)

Literacy purpose: Students interpret behavioral data by reading a graph of flight initiation distance.

  • Urban and mountain juncos respond differently to humans.
  • Urban juncos allow humans to get closer before flying away.
  • Students connect behavior to living near people.
Case Study B photo: junco behavior and flight initiation distance data.
Case Study C

Rats (Rattus spp.)

Literacy purpose: Students interpret a mutation table and connect DNA changes to poison resistance.

  • Rats near humans may face poison as a selection pressure.
  • Some mutations may help rats survive exposure.
  • Students link DNA, proteins, traits, and survival.
Case Study C photo: rat background reading and mutation table.
Disciplinary literacy strategy: Students read across multiple representations: narrative text, organism images, graphs, tables, and captions. The teacher should help students move from “I read it” to “I can use this evidence to explain a pattern.”
4

Modeling the Case Studies

Additional literacy connections in modeling

  • Students translate written and visual information into a systems model.
  • Students justify model components using evidence from the case studies.
  • Students use scientific vocabulary to explain interactions and changes over time.
  • Students revise models after discussion and new evidence.

What students are modeling

  • Urban environmental pressures: pavement, buildings, people, food waste, poison, or disturbance.
  • Organism traits or behaviors: seed type, boldness, flight distance, poison resistance.
  • Cause-and-effect relationships: which traits may help organisms survive or reproduce.
  • Population-level change over time.

Teacher guidance

A scientific model is not just a drawing. It is a thinking tool. Students use arrows, labels, and evidence notes to show how parts of the system interact.

Ask: “What is interacting?” “What changes?” “What evidence supports that arrow?”

5

Question Formulation Technique and Driving Question Board

Scientific Discourse Skills

As students build the DQB, they practice speaking, listening, paraphrasing, clarifying, and refining ideas collaboratively. Students learn that scientific understanding grows through questioning and discussion.

What QFT does in this lesson

Students use the case studies to generate questions instead of waiting for the teacher to provide the next question. This makes the unit storyline more student-driven.

  • Generate many questions.
  • Revise open and closed questions.
  • Categorize questions by theme.
  • Prioritize questions that help explain the phenomenon.

DQB Artifact

DQB photo: student questions organized into categories.
Disciplinary literacy strategy: Students practice asking scientific questions that can guide investigation. The board also makes student thinking visible to the teacher.
6

Hawksbeard Simulation Data Collection

Investigation Question

Why does hawksbeard produce a different proportion of seed types in urban and non-urban environments?

What students practice

  • Collecting data in organized tables.
  • Comparing starting seeds to surviving seeds.
  • Calculating proportions.
  • Using survival data to explain why one seed type may become more common.
Data literacy connection: Students organize evidence into tables, compare proportions, identify patterns, and make evidence-based claims from quantitative data.
Math-literacy connection: The proportion calculation helps students turn counts into comparable evidence.

Data Collection Photos

Non-Urban Trial data collection sheet.
Urban Trial data collection sheet.
7

Reading the Garden Experiment Like Scientists

Scientific Reading Practices

  • Students compare classroom-generated evidence with professional scientific research.
  • Students analyze experimental design and identify controlled variables.
  • Students evaluate how evidence supports a scientific explanation.
  • Students distinguish between simulation limitations and scientific conclusions.

Purpose of this reading

Students compare their classroom simulation to a real scientific study. They decide whether their simulation supports the same kind of explanation scientists developed from authentic hawksbeard data.

Instructional goal: Students analyze the real-life Garden Experiment to see how scientists collect evidence, control variables, and explain patterns in populations over time. By comparing their own simulation data to the scientific study, students evaluate whether their classroom investigation supports similar conclusions about why non-feathery seeds may become more common in urban environments.

Common Garden Setup

Scientists grew plants under controlled conditions so they could compare seed production without the immediate effects of different natural environments.

Experiment 1

Scientists recreated urban seed dispersal conditions and compared the proportion of seed types before and after seeds landed in artificial urban plots.

Experiment 2

Scientists compared whether plants from urban and non-urban parent populations produced different proportions of seed types.

Garden Experiment Artifact

Garden Experiment photo: common garden setup and Experiment 1 reading. Click to enlarge.

Analysis Questions Artifact

Analysis Question 1.
Analysis Questions 2–4.

Teacher facilitation notes

  • Question 1: Students compare their investigation to Experiment 1. Look for “same pattern, different level of control or realism.”
  • Question 2: Students should wonder how feathery-seed parents can produce some non-feathery seeds and what this suggests about inherited variation.
  • Question 3: Students connect common garden evidence to the idea that differences are not only caused by immediate growing conditions.
  • Question 4: Students predict population change over generations using survival and reproduction reasoning.
8

Teacher Look-Fors

Disciplinary literacy across the lesson: Students read scientific texts, interpret visuals and quantitative data, engage in evidence-based discussion, ask investigable questions, model systems, compare explanations, and communicate claims using scientific reasoning.
Case Study Reading: Students cite a graph, table, or text detail when explaining how urban and non-urban populations differ.
Jigsaw Talk: Students teach their case study using evidence, not just organism facts.
Modeling: Student models include interactions and arrows that show cause-and-effect relationships.
DQB: Student questions are connected to the phenomenon and sorted into meaningful categories.
Simulation Data: Students calculate proportions and use them to compare seed survival in different environments.
Garden Experiment Comparison: Students explain how a classroom simulation can support, but not fully replace, evidence from a controlled scientific study.
Bottom line for teachers: These lessons are designed to help students practice science-specific reading, writing, speaking, and modeling. The literacy work is not separate from the science; it is how students make sense of the science.
